def: s.f. Firewood.
adj. See s.v. hata or hat. s.m. (contrac. of iḥātā, q.v.), Premises, an enclosure, court-yard, 'compound' (in Anglo-Indian phraseology).
s.m. (dialec.) Ware, goods.
n. f. ﮩتک rending (a veil, etc). See be-izzatī. hatak izzat k. , v. a. To bring disgrace upon; to defame. hatak izzat yā hurmat , Defamation of character.
adj. Golden; — s.m. Gold
subs: s.m. A golden pot or jar

def: v. n. 1. To be sent or driven back or repulsed. 2. To be backward; to hesitate. Merā jī in bātoṅ se haṭaktā hai .

ety: haṭak˚, prob. = Prk. हटक्के(इ), fr. S. हृत (p.p.p. of rt. हृ)+कृ
def: v.n. To be sent or driven back, to be repulsed; to be checked or stopped; to retreat; — to move; — to stop; — to be backward; to have a misgiving; to hesitate, to shrink (from, -se), be averse (to); to boggle.
more: v. n. To be repulsed, to be driven back, to retreat, move, stop, boggle.

def: s.m. The act of slaying, &c. (see hatnā).
v. a. To remove. See ﮩتَنا v. n.
v.t. To take away, to remove; to move out of the way, put back, put aside; — to cause to recede, to drive back or off, to repel; to worst, foil; — to put off, postpone
more: (causal of هٿنا) v. a. To repel, drive backwards, to back, to worst, to foil.
subs: v.t. (intens.) To move aside or out of the way; — to drive back, to repel, repulse, &c. (see haṭānā).
wety: Causative form of ہٹنا .

def: (from هٿنا) s. (dakh.) Falling back, retiring, flinching.
adj. Of or relating to the market; — marketable; — (dialec.) low, mean, dirty.
s.m. = H هتوڙي हतौड़ी hatauṛī, s.f. = hathauṛā, and hathauṛī, qq.v.
n. m. hathauṛī , n. f. H. hāth hand, toṛnā to break. A hammer.
s.f. = H هتوڙا हतौड़ा hatauṛā, s.m. = hathauṛā, and hathauṛī, qq.v.
n. f. Body; frame.
s.f. Body; frame.
s.f. Skill of hand, &c. (i.q. hathauṭī, q.v.); — (dialec.) a smith's small hammer (i.q. hatauṛī or hathauṛī).

ety: haṭ(nā) and haṭā(nā)+āw = Prk. एअव्वं = S. (इ)तव्यं
def: s.m. Receding; falling back, retiring, retreating, retreat; flinching; shrinking (from), aversion (from or to); — removal; dismission; repelling, repulse (of an army).

n. m. S. हस्त Pr. hattho ; A. yad . 1. The hand; arm. 1. Hāth ko hāth paichhāntā hai . Prov. Hand knoweth hand. (The creditor knows his debtor). 2. Lāṭhī hāth kī, bhāī sāth kā . Prov. A club in the hand, a brother nigh. 3. Maulā hāth baṛhāiāṅ, jis chāheṅ tis deṅ . Prov. Advancement comes from God, he gives to whom He wills. 4. Āge hāth, pīchhe pāt . Prov. Before her her hands, behind but leaves. (She is so poor that she has to cover her pudendum with her hands and her back with leaves). 5. Hān, lābh, jīvan, maran, jas, apjas Bidhi hāth . Prov. Loss and gain, life and death, honor and disgrace in God's hands are. 6. Bāman se bāman mile kar-kar lambe hāth, Den len ko kuchh nahīṅ, namaskār kī bāt . When Brahmans meet hands are stretched free, Transactions none, mere courtesy. 7. Hāth māṅ nā gāt māṅ, 'main dhanvantī jāt māṅ .' Prov. Not a rap she has got, no jewels her grace: 'I'm of a wealthy high-born race.' 8. Hathiyā chale na paīyāṅ, baiṭhe de, Gusaiyāṅ! Prov. Nor hand nor foot can I e'en move, my food send me, O God above! 2. A cubit measure; half a yard. Do hāth kā ek gaz . 3. A hand (in an Office, etc.). Āj kal hāth kam ho gaë haiṅ . (Artizans). 4. Reach. 5. Possession; clutches. 6. Protection; patronage. 7. Power. Ab tak to yeh bāt hamāre hāth meṅ hai . 8. A hand in cards. hāth, hāthoṅ , adv. By the hands of; through. hāth utarnā , v. n. To be dislocated (one's hand). hāth uṭhā-ke denā , v. a. 1. To give with one's hand. 2. To give reluctantly. hāth uṭhā-ke kosnā . To call down curses on one. Hāth uṭhā-kar woh kosne jo lage, Wāh re! ham use duā samjhe! Mukhaīyar. With hands upraised she called down curses on my head, How excellent! for blessings I took them instead. hāth uṭhānā , v. n. 1. To raise or lift up one's hands; to salute. 2. To lift up the hands in prayer; to pray for one. 3. To lift one's hands against one. Bīvī par hāth uṭhātā hai . 4. ( hāth dhonā ) To wash one's hands of; to keep from; desist; abstain; refrain or withdraw from; resign ( dast-bardar h .). Hāth uṭhā jaur aur jafā se tū! Ye hī goyā salām hai terā . Your hands from tyranny withdraw, this for your salutation I'll receive. 5. To give up hope; to despair. hāth ānā, paṛnā, lagnā, yā chaṛhnā, hāth meṅ ānā ; Sant. hatāo , 1. To come to hand; fall to one; come into one's possession or power. 1. Hāth lag-jānā shart hai . Provided I can manage to come by it. 2. Ankar bāchha chāṭaī gāë, bhari muṅh bār hāthai āë Bhoj. Prov. If a cow another's calf should lick no gain is hers save hairs that stick. 3. Kesrī kes, pataṅg maṛīṅ, satī kuch, gaj dant, Sūr kaṭārī, bipr dhan, mare pailāge hast . The lion's mane, the jewel in snake's head, The Sati's breasts, ivory in elephant bred, The brave man's sword, the Brahman's wealth hoarded, No hand can ever touch till they are dead. 4. Sākh gaë phir hāth na āë . Prov. No credit lost is e'er regained. 2. To gain; accrue ( hāsil h ). Bair rakhe jo sā dhū sāth kachhu na āve vāke hāth . Prov. Who show t'wards saints hostility no gain their hands will e'er come by. hāth ūṅchā h , v. n. To be able to give; to be well off. hāth bāṅdhnā , v. n. To join the hands in supplication. hāth bāṅdhe khaṛe rahnā, hāth bāṅdhe rahnā , v. n. To wait upon; to attend. hāth baṛhānā, yā chalānā ; Sant. tī tīar , v. n. 1. To stretch one's hands towards. 2. To hand or pass anything. 3. ( hāth dauṛānā ) To make a long arm. 4. To exceed bounds; encroach upon. hāth bikānā , v. n. To be sold or made over to. Jog kīyā, par jugatī na jānā, Jam Rājā ke hāth bikānā . Kabir. hāth band yā taṅg h. , v. n. To be straitened or poor. hāth bharnā , v. n. 1. To be fatigued (the hands). 2. To be daubed or smeared (one's hands). hāth baiṭhnā yā jamnā , v. n. 1. To be set or used to; to be settled (one's hand); to have one's hand in. 2. To be driven home; to be effective. hāth pānī lenā , v. n. See āb-dast k . hāth pāoṅ bachānā , v. n. To protect oneself; to look out. hāth pāoṅ phūlnā , v. n. lit . to be swelled (the hands and feet). To be confounded. hāth pāoṅ tharrānā , v. n. To tremble or shake all over. hāth pāoṅ toṛnā , v. n. To maim. hāth pāoṅ ṭūṭnā , v. a. 1. To be broken (hands and feet). 2. To ache (the joints). hāth pāoṅ ṭhanḍe h . v. n. To become cold (the hands and feet). hāth pāoṅ chalnā , v. a. To be able-bodied, or able to work. Itnā to piṭ līyā, magar hāth pāoṅ chale hī jāte haiṅ! He has had such a beating, yet his hands and feet still go! hāth pāoṅ se chhuṭnā , Wom. v. n. To have a safe delivery. hāth pāoṅ mārnā, yā hilānā , v. a. 1. To throw out or work one's hands and legs. 2. To strike out (in swimming). 3. To throw one's arms about and jump in agony. 4. To strive hard; to struggle. 5. To work; labor. 1. Hāth pāoṅ hilā! Bhagvān degā . Prov. Stir your arms and legs and God will give. 2. Be-hāth pāoṅ hilāë kuchh nahīṅ hotā . Prov. Nothing can be done without stirring one's hands and feet. hāth pāoṅ nikālnā , v. a. 1. To be well-developed (the body). 2. To begin to show off. 3. To begin one's pranks. hāth pāoṅ hārnā , v. n. To lose courage; to be dispirited. hāth patthar tale dabnā , v. n. 1. To have the hands crushed under a stone. 2. To be entangled in a difficulty. 3. To be helpless, or unable to act. 4. To be brought to a dead stop. hāth par Qurān, yā Gaṅgā jalī rakhnā , v. a. To make one swear by the Quran or on Ganges' water; administer an oath. hāth par nāg khilānā , v. a. To risk one's life. Parāë bachchoṅ kā pālnā hāth pe nāg khilānā hai . To bring up another's child is to feed a serpent. hāth par hāth dhare baiṭhnā yā baiṭhe rahnā , To sit with folded hands; to do nothing. hāth par hāth mārnā , v. n. 1. To join hands in token of confirming a promise; to make a promise. 1. Bachan do, aur hāth pe hāth māro! Give me your word and strike your hands together! 2. Tab mār-kar uske hāth par hāth, Bolā ki hai qaul jān ke sāth . Nasīm. 2. To bet; pledge oneself. hāth paṛnā , v. n. To be plundered, looted. Āj bāzār meṅ hāth paṛ gayā. hāth pasārnā, yā phailānā , v. n. 1. To stretch out one's hands. Jahāṅ chār ādmī baiṭhe dekhe hāth phailā diye . Wherever four men sit hands (of beggars) stretched forth are. 2. To beg. hāth pasāre jānā , v. n. To go from this world empty-handed. Māl jinhoṅ ne jamā kīyā voh hāth pasāre jāte haiṅ . Who wealth have stored on earth with hands outstretched have gone out just. hāth pakaṛnā , v. n. 1. To take by the hands. 2. To take in hand or protect one. hāth phernā , v. n. 1. To stroke; caress. 2. To fleece; rob; ease of one's money. hāth pheṅknā , To brandish; wield (a sword, etc.). hāth pīle k. , Hin. v. n. lit . to stain the hands with turmeric. To get one married; have a quiet or inexpensive wedding. hāth taknā , v. n. 1. To look wistfully at one's hand. 2. To depend upon another for subsistence. hāth tale , Under one's hand. hāth thirkānā, yā nachānā , v. n. To dance one's hands; to gesticulate. hāth joṛnā ; Sant. joṛ hāt , v. a. 1. To join both hands (in supplication). 1. Kar gujrān garībi se, abhimānā kis par kartā hai? Kabīr. Jogī ho-ke jaṭā rakhāī, naṅge pāoṅ phirtā hai, Uṅchī bhujā kar hāth joṛtā, jo kyā Ishvar aṅdhā hai? Walk thou with true humility, For what hath pride to do with thee? Bare-foot, hair-matted hanging low, Doth God delight, is God served so? Why lift your hands both joined so high? Is God then blind, not ever nigh? 2. Koṛī nā māne hāthā-joṛī . Rus. The bribe taker will have you pay, beg hard with hands joined as you may. 2. To pay reverence or homage to. 3. To hold in fear or awe. Bas! tum ko hāth joṛtā hūṅ! Ironic. hāth jhāṛnā , v. n. 1. To give one a beating. 2. To strike a blow. Itnā sun kuṅjṛā uṭh dauṛā, līye hāth meṅ lāṭhī, Kuṅjṛan bhī dauṛī jhat se, līye hāth lukāṭhī, Hāth kuṅjṛe par jhāṛā . Kuṅjṛan kī laṛāī. hāth jhāṛ-ke khaṛā ho jānā , v. n. To stand up empty-handed; to empty one's purse. hāth jhaṭaknā , v. a. To draw away one's hand with a jerk. hāth jhūṭā k. , v. a. See ulush k . hāth jhūṭhā h. , v. n. 1. To be rendered unserviceable (one's hand). 2. To be smeared or dirty (one's hand with food). hāth-chālāk ; Illit. hath-chalāk, hath-chalā ; Sant. hāth-chuṭī , adj. 1. Light-fingered. 2. Dextrous; deft; nimble; skilful. hāth-chālākī , n. f. 1. Dexterity; sleight of hand; expertness. 2. Thievishness. hāth chalānā , v. a. 1. To use one's hands freely; to strike; beat. 2. To pass one's hands over. Āṅkheṅ parī-rukhoṅ se laṛātī haiṅ roṭiyāṅ, Sīnah upar bhī hāth chalāti haiṅ roṭiyāṅ . Nazīr. When bread their stomachs fills at fair ones they make eyes, And bread-fed lust on breasts its fingers plies. hāth chūmnā , v. n. To kiss one's hand reverentially. hāth chhoṛnā , v. a. To give a sword cut; to strike. hāth-khālī , adj. Empty-handed; penniless. hāth khālī nah., hāth kām meṅh . To have one's hand in; to be engaged; to have no leisure. hāth khālī nahīṅ hai . (Said to beggars). hāth khālī jānā , To have no picture card in a hand. hāth dikhānā , v. a. 1. To show one's palm (to a fortune-teller). 2. To show one's pulse to a physician. hāth dharnā, yā rakhnā , v. a. 1. To take by the hand. 2. To support; maintain; patronize; protect. hāth dho-ke pīchhe paṛnā , v. n. 1. To pursue closely in spite of every thing. 2. To persecute. hāth dhonā, hāth dho baiṭhnā , v. n. 1. ( hāth dhonā ) To wash one's hands. 2. To wash one's hands of. 3. To be despondent. Ham to apnī jān se hāth dho baiṭhe . hāth dekhnā , v. a. 1. To feel one's pulse. 2. To practise palmistry; tell one's fortunes. hāth denā , v. a. 1. To give one's hand to. 2. ( Horse-dealers and jewellers ) To make a secret bargain with the hands under a cloth. 3. To pledge oneself to. 4. To support a cause. 5. To put out a light. 6. See bāg moṛnā , 2. Mātā rānī hāth de gaī . 7. To as certain if one is possessed by an evil spirit by feeling his hand. 8. To exorcise. hāth ḍālnā , v. a. 1. ( hāth lagānā ) To thrust or put one's hands in. Khālā kī mehmānī, hāth ḍāl pachhtānī . Prov. Your aunt's hospitality's patent, put your hand in her dish and repent. 2. To touch or insult a woman. 3. To meddle; interfere in. 4. To plunder. hāth-ḍanḍī , n. f. A pair of hand scales. hāth roknā , 1. To hold or stay one's hand. 2. ( hāth sukeṛnā yā sameṭnā ) To keep back; withhold. 3. ( hāth kheṅchnā ) To use sparingly. hāth raṅgnā , v. n. 1. To dye one's hands with henna. 2. To be greased (one's palm); take bribes. hāth sādhnā, yā sāf k . v. a. To form one's hand. hāth sir par rakhnā , v. a. 1. To take under one's protection; to patronize. 2. To swear by one's head. hāth se . By the hands of; by; through. hāth se nikalnā , v. a. 1. To pass through one's hands. 2. To slip through one's fingers. hāth se de baiṭhnā, yā denā , v. a. To give away; bestow. Ātā ho to hāth se na dīje, Jātā ho to uskā gam na kīje! Prov. What comes to you let not your hands let go, Let not what goes from you your heart make low! hāth se gayā , adj. Lost; flown. hāth sāf k. , v. n. 1. To beat; trounce. 2. To cut one down. 3. To slay. 4. To fleece; plunder. 5. To practise hand-writing. hāth kāṭ denā , v. a. 1. To cut off one's hand. 2. To pledge or bind oneself. hāth kā jhūṭā . An unreliable or dishonest person. hāth-kā-dīyā . A gift; donation. Hāth kā dīyā sāth chale . Prov. Whate'er your hands have given will go with you to Heaven. hāth kā sachchā , adj. Honest; trustworthy. hāth kā mail , n. f. Vile trash; filthy lucre. Rupayā, paisā hāth kā mail hai. hāth kāṅpnā, yā tharrānā , v. n. To tremble (one's hand). hāth kaṭnā , v. n. 1. To be cut off (one's hand). 2. To be bound to. hāth k. , v. a. To cut at one ( wār k ). hāth khujlānā , v. n. To itch (the hands, to beat one). hāth kholnā , v. a. To be open-handed. hath kī chiṭṭhī , n. f. 1. A letter under one's own hand. 2. A receipt. hāth kī lakīreṅ ; Sant. tiol . 1. The lines on the palms of one's hands. 2. Fate. hāth ke nīche ānā, hāth tale ānā , v. n. To come under one's clutches. hāth-gāṛī , n. f. A hand-barrow. hāth-lapak . Light-fingered gentry. hāth lagānā , v. a. 1. To touch ( chhūnā , 1). Dekhat kī ek bhūrī bhārī, hāth lagāvat hove kārī . Riddle. Ans. Missī . To sight it's brown, touch it it's black. 2. To lay one's hands on. 1. Mere bachche ko hāth lagāyā to tum hī jāno! If you lay hands on my child, look out! 2. Jo tauṅh hāṅs ghaṛāve nāhīṅ tau tohe saṅg na soūṅgī, Khet kār-ke hāth nā lāūṅ, nā terī ṛoṭī poūṅgī . Rus. Wom. song. If you a necklace wont give me your bed I will not share, A bit of field-work I'll do ne'er, nor food for you prepare. 3. To put one's hand to; to begin (a work). hāth lagnā , v. n. 1. To come to hand ( hāth ānā ). 2. ( Arith .) To carry. Pandrah ke pāṅch hāth lagā ek. hāth lage mailā h . To be so pure and bright that the slightest touch would soil it. hāth mārnā , v. a. 1. See hāth sāf k . 3, 4, and hāth par hāth mārnā (1), 2. To pilfer; purloin; embezzle. 3. To play a good knife and fork. 4. To swallow large morsels quickly. hāth milānā , v. a. 1. To join or shake hands. 2. To join hands before wrestling. 3. To fight (hand with hand). 4. ( Shopkeepers ) To make a purchase. 5. To give alms. Āj ikādshī hai, kuchh hāth se hāth milāo! Brahmins. hāth malnā , v. n. 1. To wring one's hands. 1. Supne meṅ suāmī āë, kah na sakī ek bāt, Sovat thī, rovat uṭhī, mal mal rah gaī hāth! My spouse came to me in a dream, I naught to him could say, I slept all through, I weeping woke, my hands I wringing lay. 2. Yeh woh zamīṅ thī zamīṅ jiskī zar ugaltī thī, Yeh khāk voh thī kī aksīr hāth maltī thī , Fugān-i-Dehlī. Zahīr. A country this in history famed, whose soil once belched up gold, Its dust gold-dust which alchemists with envy did behold. hāth meṅ ṭhīkrā denā , v. a. To reduce one to beggary. hāth meṅ ṭhīkrā lenā , v. n. To take to begging; to beg. Aisā meṅh barsegā ki ṭhīkrā hāth meṅ līye phiroge . Soothsayers. It will rain so that you will go about with a shard in your hand. (1. You will have to bale out the water. 2. You will become a beggar). hāth meṅ dil rakhnā , v. a. To have a regard for one; to gratify every wish. hāth meṅ lānā , v. n. 1. To bring into one's hand. Hāth meṅ lānā, pāt meṅ khānā . Prov. Come to your hand, eaten on a leaf. (To live from hand to mouth). 2. To bring under. hāth meṅ lenā , v. a. To take in one hands; grasp; hold. hāth meṅ līye phirnā , v. n. To be randy. Phr. To carry one's J. T. in his hand. hāth meṅ hāth denā , v. a. To give one's hand in marriage. Tumhārā bāp aisā nādān nahīṅ ki tumhārā hāth ek gair ādmī ke hāth meṅ de de . 2. To hand over; give in one's charge. 3. To take one's hand to put one's hand in another's. hāth mei hunar h. , v. n. To be skilled in any handicraft or art. Jiske hāth meṅ hunar hai voh bhūkā nahīṅ maregā . Whose skill in handicraft doth lie of lunger he will never die. hāth na rakhne denā, puṭṭhe par hāth na dharne denā , v. n. 1. To shrink from the touch. 2. To be firm or unyielding. Id ā jo sirā hai to koī dukān-dār hāth nahīṅ charne detā . Bebrs Id prices rise so much there's not a thing you can e'en touch. 3. To be high-mettled, spirited (a horse). hāth hilīte, pallā jhulāte ānā , v. n. To come sweeping along without a penny to one's name. Kaṛī na lāë, hāth hilāte pallā jhulāte chale āë . Not a farthing he has brought, swinging his arms and flapping his sides he strides for naught. hāth hilië jānā , v. n. To go on eating. hāthoṅ chāoṅ rakhnā, yā k. , v. a. To ake under one's wing. hāthoṅ senikalnā , v. n. 1. To pass through one's hands. 2. To escape from one's control. hāthoṅ kaejah uchhalnā , v. n. To palpitate (the heart). hāthoṅ mei rakhnā , v. a. To bring up tenderly. hāthoṅ hāt , adv. 1. From hand to hand; out of hand. Harī raābī, chiṭṭā bhāt, Lo re pacho hāthoṅ hāth! Riddle. Ans. Chambelī . Jasmine. 2. Quiclly; expeditiously. hāthoṅ hāth ur jānā, yā biknā , v. n. To find a ready sale. hāthoṅ hāth lejānā , v. n. To carry away quickly. hāthoṅ hāth lenā , To receive one with respect. apne ūpar hāth chalānā , v. n. To lay violent hands upon oneself; to attempt or commit suicide. ulṭā hāth mārnā , v. a. To give one a back-hander. donoṅ hāthoṅ sameṭnā , v. a. lit . To collect with both hands. To coin money. kālak ke hāth lagānā , v. a. To bring disgrace on one. kisī ke hāth bhejnā , v. a. To send by some one. kisī ke hāth bechnā , v. a. To sell to some one.

Cont. of ﮩـاتهـ q. v. hath-udhār . See dast-gardāṅ , 1. hath udhār, yā hath pher denā . To lend for use. hath-pher , n. f. 1. Sleight of hand (in a money changer); changing a good for a bad rupee. 2. E. Borrowing. 3. Fondling; caress. hath-pher k. , v. a. 1. To caress. 2. To embezzle. 3. To gulp down. hath pher lenā , E. v. n. To borrow. hath-chhuṭ ; Brij, hath chhuṭkū , One who is in the habit of striking others. hath-ras , n. 1. Amorous dalliance. 2. Masturbation. Hai hāth kī garmī meṅ jo az-bas kī bharā ras, Is wāste kahte haiṅ sab is kām ko hath-ras . Nazīr. hath-kaṭī , n. f. A sword-cut which is intended to take off or disable the adversary's hand. hath-kaṛī , n. f. A handcuff; manacle. hath-khaṅḍe , n. m. 1. Manual dexterity. 2. Legerdemain. hath-levā , n. m. A part of the Hin. marriage ceremony. The hands of the bride and bridegroom are joined palm to palm with some flour put between them, and tied with a red thread. hattoṅ se jānā , v. n. To cut and run. hat'thī, hatho , W. Rus. 1. A stick for stirring up the juice of the sugarcane. 2. A hair glove for cleaning horses. hatte chaṛhnā , v. n. To come under one's power. hathyā'na, hatyānā , v. n. To appropriate; nab; obtain by fraud. Ser chūn maṅg kanglā lāyā, chhīn paṛausan ne hatyāyā . Rus. Prov. A seer of flour a pauper begged and brought, a neighbour grabbed and left her naught. (One does the work, another draws the pay). hath'lī, hathrī, haithī , n. f. The winch or handle of a spinning-wheel. hatauṭ'ī , n. f. 1. Mode of handling or wielding. 2. Skill; dexterity. hat'thā , A large bunch (of plantains). Ek hatthā kelā aur thorā sā thakūā maiṅ ne chhaṭh meṅ buā ko bhejā hai . Tir. hatho'nā, hathaunā , Sweet cakes put in the hands of the bride and bridegroom.

s.m. The hand; arm; — paw; trunk (of an elephant); — a cubit (measure); — a hand (in an office, or a factory, &c.); — a hand (at cards); — a blow, slap; — (met.) reach; possession, clutches; power; protection, patronage; — adv. By the hands (of, kisī-ke), by, through; — to (e.g. kisī-ke hāth de-denā or beć-denā, 'to make over to, or to sell to, any one)
more: s. m. 1. The hand. 2. A cubit. 3. Possession, power. هاتهہ آنا hāth ānā, To come into oneʼs possession or power, to be gained or obtained. هاتهہ اٿهانا hāth uṭhānā, 1. To leave off, refrain from, desist, abandon, relinquish, resign, cease. 2. To salute by raising the hand to the head. Both these meanings are alluded to in the following example. Muṣt̤afạ̄ ḵẖān Yakrang says: هاتهہ اٿها جوراورجفا سي تو يہي گويا سلام هي تيرا Hāth uṭhā jaur aur jafā se tū; yih-ī goyā salām hai terā. Restrain thy hands from violence and injustice; this is thy salutation. 3. To beat. 4. To give alms. هاتهہ اوٿ لينا hāth oṭ lenā, To receive with both hands open and stretched out together. هاتهہ باندهنا hāth-bāndhnā, To join the hands in a supplicating posture. هاتهہ بڙهانا hāth baṛhānā, 1. To endeavour to get any thing. 2. To gain possession of the property of others. هاتهہ بند هونا hāth band honā, 1. To be much…
subs: v.n. To come to hand, to reach, be received; to come into the possession, or power (of, -ke, or mere, &c.); to fall (to); to be gained, be obtained, be found
v.n. The hand to be dislocated
(-ko), To give with the hand; — to give reluctantly
To raise the hand or hands (in salutation, or prayer, &c.), to salute; to pray (for); to give alms; — to raise the hand (against, -par), to strike, beat

(-se), To withdraw the hand (from); to keep the hands off; to leave off, cease, desist (from), refrain (from), keep (from); to abandon, forego, relinquish, renounce, resign; — to release, let go, set at liberty (i.q. P. dast bar-dāshtan); — to give up hope (of), to despair (i.q. hāth dhonā)
v.t. To take or receive with both hands open and stretched out together
v.n. 'The hand (of a person) to be raised'; to be in a position (or to be able) to give; to be well-off
To join the hands in a supplicating posture
v.n. 'To remain standing with hands joined,' to continue standing in attendance (on), to wait upon

To stretch out the hand (towards, or for); to endeavour to get (anything); to hand or pass (a thing); — to gain possession of the property (of another); to encroach (upon), to exceed bounds (i.q. hāth dauṛānā)
To be sold or made over (to)
'The hands (of a person) to be tied'; to be in straitened circumstances, to be poor or helpless; — to be much engaged in business, to have no leisure
v.n. The hands to become wearied or fatigued; — the hands to be covered or stained (or daubed or smeared, by or with)
or baiṭh-jānā, v.n. The hand to be set or used (to); to have the hand in practice, to acquire perfection by practice (in any art, &c.); — to be driven home, to be effective
v.t. To sell or dispose of (to another)
or hāth-pāṅwoṅ, or hāth-pāʼoṅ, s.m. Hand and foot; hands and feet; — a right-hand man, a factotum
(-ke), To tie the hands and feet (of), to bind (one) hand and foot
To protect oneself, to look out
The hands and feet to be or become swollen; — to be or become distressed or confounded
To extend (one's) business or schemes; — to obtain (things) fraudulently; to take bribes
The hands and legs to tremble; to tremble or shake all over
(-ke), To break the hands and feet (of); to maim; to cripple
The hands and feet to break or be broken; — to suffer breaking pains in the limbs (as in incipient fever, &c.)
v.n. The limbs to be movable or active; to be able-bodied, to be able to work
To wash the hands and feet; — to go to the necessary
To get off safe and sound; — to have a safe delivery

To throw about the hands and legs; to work the hands and legs; to sprawl; to flounder; — to strike out (in swimming); — to throw the arms about and jump about (in agony); — to be agitated; — to strive hard, struggle, endeavour, strain, toil, labour

'To put forth the hands and feet'; to begin to show off; — to begin one's pranks; to pursue a loose (or an evil) course of conduct; to show one's teeth; — the limbs (or the body) to become well-developed
v.n. To lose courage, to become dispirited
'The hand to be crushed under a stone'; to be entangled in a difficulty; — to be helpless, to be unable to act; — to be brought to a dead stop
adv. On the hand; — on the nail
To be placed on the hand (ready for another to take); to be in readiness (for another to take or accept)
(or gaṅgā-jalī) rakhnā, 'To put the Qorʼān (or Ganges-water) in the hands' (of anyone, to swear by); to make one swear by the Qorʼān, &c.; to administer an oath (to)
'To feed a serpent on (one's) hand'; to place life in jeopardy, to risk life
hāth-par hāth-dhare baiṭhnā or baiṭhe rahnā
To sit (or to continue sitting) with folded hands; to do nothing
To strike the hands together; to wring the hands (in grief, or despair, &c.); — to join hands (in token of confirming a promise, &c.); to make a promise; to pledge oneself (to); to bet
To fall into the hands (of), to come into the possession (of, -ke, or mere, &c.); — the hand (of another) to fail (on one), to be robbed or plundered (e.g. āj bāzār-meṅ hāth paṛ-gayā)
or hāth phailānā, To stretch or hold out the hand; to ask (for), to beg
To seize or take the hand (of, -kā, or merā, &c.), to take by the hand; to hand (a person); to shake hands; — to protect, aid, foster
To lead away by the hand; to hand (a person)
s.m. A kind of plant, Pothos
(-par), To pass the hand (over); to feel; to rub, stroke, caress; — to retouch; — to fleece, rob
To give on loan, to lend
To brandish, to wield (a sword, &c.); to fence
(-ke), 'To stain the hands (of a girl) with turmeric,' to get (a girl) married; to have a quiet or inexpensive wedding
(-kā), To look wistfully at the hand (of); — to depend upon (another) for subsistence
(-ke, or mere, &c.), To come under the hand (of), to come into the power or clutches (of)
or hāth naćānā, To shake or wave the hands, to gesticulate
(conj. part. of next), adv. With the hands folded in the manner of entreaty

v.n. To join or clasp the hands (in humility, or entreaty, &c.); to pay reverence or homage (to, -ko); to bow (to), submit (to), to knuckle down; to hold in fear or awe; — to entreat earnestly, to supplicate
(-par), To let fly or drive (at), to strike (at); to strike a blow; to beat; — to empty the hand (of money, &c.), to give, bestow

n. m. S. हस्त The 13th mansion of the moon; Corvus. 1. Hathiyā barse tīn hot haiṅ, shakkar, shālī, māsh, Hathiyā barse tīn jāt haiṅ, tillī, kodo, kapās . When in the thirteenth mansion rain doth fall Rice, sugarcane, and pulse, these three thrive all, But cotton, kodo , sesamum, all three The rains destroy beyond all remedy. 2. Chaṛhte barse ardarā, utrat barse hast, Kitnau Rājā ḍāṅṛ le rahe anand girkast . If in the sixth rain falls and in the thirteenth mansion too, Whate'er the king may take the peasantry are well-to-do. 3. Hathiyā barse, chitrā maṅḍrāë, ghar baiṭhe kisān ririyāë . Bhoj. Prov. Rains in the thirteenth mansiou, and the next with clouds o'erhung, The peasantry sit in their homes with grief and sorrow wrung.
more: n. f. S. हन् kill. 1. Slaughter; murder. 2. A sin. 1. Chhoṭī sī bachhiyā baṛī sī hattyā . Prov. The calf killed is so small, the sin so great withal. 2. Khassī mār gharvaiyā khāë, hattiyā le le pāhun jāë . Prov. The household all dine on the goat that's slain, The sin of slaughter on the guest's head lain. 3. Plague; pest. Udhār baṛī hattyā hai . Prov. Borrowing is a plague. 4. A very weak or sick person. Is hattyā se kyā laṛūṅ? 5. A quarrel. Hattyā kā mūl hai . hattyā palle baṅdhnā, yā mol lenā , v. n. To be nailed to a cross. hattyā k. , v. a. 1. To kill; murder. 2. To commit a sin.

ety: act. part. n. of حتم 'to decree,' &c.
def: s.m. One who decrees or ordains; one who pronounces a judicial decision, a judge; — a black crow, a raven (so called because its croaking is held to necessitate separation or departure); — the name of an Arabian chief (surnamed Tāʼī, from his tribe t̤ai) famed for his boundless liberality; — (hence) adj. & s.m. Liberal, generous, bountiful; — a liberal or generous man.

n. m. The name of an Arab chief, famed for his boundless liberality; hence, a liberal, generous man. 1. Merī Hātam māī, bhejyo! Mendicants' cry. Send me out a crust, Hatim mother! 2. Yeh bhī apne vaqt ke Hātam haiṅ . Prov. Saying. He is the Hatim of the age. 3. Gor par rotī hai Hātam ki sakhāvat in dinoṅ . Charity now weeps at Hātim's grave. 4. Hātam kī gor par lāt mārī . Prov. He has kicked Hātim's grave. (He puts Hatim's liberality to shame). (Said ironically of a miser). 5. Bakhīloṅ kī ba-daulat rah gayā hai nām Hātim kā . Nāsikh. Through misers Hātim has got a name.
more: (from حتم Heb. To seal, to sign, to confirm, to decide, to judge) part. act. used substantively, 1. A judge. 2. A black crow (probably, from decisions being formed by augury from that bird). 3. The proper name of a most liberal Arabian, surnamed طاٴيي from his tribe طيّ; whence 4. ( met. ) adj. Liberal, generous, bountiful.

Kal mai-kade meṅ ham jo gaë bā qad-i-dutā, Aur pī sharāb loṭ gaë, bā shor o gul machā, Us dam hamāre dekh buṛhāpe kā hauslā, Haṅs-haṅs-ke jab to pīr-i-mugāṅ ne yehī kahā, 'Pīre ki dam ze ishq zanad bas ganīmat ast .' Pahle itnā hauslah to paidā karo! First get capacity enough for your ambitious aim! 2. Courage; spirit; boldness; pluck. hausle se bāhar yā ziyādah , Beyond one's capacity or ambition. hauslah-mand, hausle-vālā , adj.…

def: s.m. (dialec. H.) corr. of A. احوال aḥwāl, q.v. the first letter of the Arabic, Persian, and Urdū alphabets. Its pronunciation depends on the vowel which accompanies it; e.g. ا with fatḥa (اَ) = the Devanāgarī अ = the English a, as in cedar (or = the English u in fun); ا with kasra (اِ) = the Devanāgarī इ = the English i, as in fin; ا with ẓamma (اُ) = the Devanāgarī उ = the English u, as in bull. — ا with fatḥa, followed by ا, (i.e. اَا, which is usually written آ) = the Devanāgarī आ = the English ā, as in father; ا with fatḥa, followed by the letter و, (i.e. اَو) = the Devanāgarī औ = the English au (sounded like ou in house); ا with fatḥa, followed by the letter ي, (i.e. اَي), = the Devanāgarī ऐ = the English ai, as in aisle. — ا with ẓamma, followed by the letter و, (i.e. اُو), = 1˚, the Devanāgarī ऊ = the English u, as in rule (or = the oo in pool), and, 2˚, = the Devanāgarī ओ = the English o, as in pole. — ا with kasra, followed by ي, (i.e. اِي), = 1˚, the Devanāgarī ई = the English ī, as in caprice; and, 2˚, = the Devanāgarī ए = the French e, as in fête. ا is used, — 1˚ (arithmetically, according to the Abjad, q.v.) for the number one. 2˚ (for अ, in Almanacks and Astrology) for Sunday, and Aries. 3˚ (for अ) as a name of the god Vishnu. 4˚ as an inseparable prefix to a word beginning with a consonant, to signify negation or privation: e.g. aśuddha, 'impure,' 'incorrect.' As in Greek, a becomes an before a vowel; e.g. ananta, 'without end,' 'endless.'

def: adj. Present, in attendance, at hand, ready, prepared, at the service of, willing, content; (in Gram.) the second person
more: adj. حضر was present. 1. Present; in attendance. 1. Hāzir hāzir, gāyab gāyab . Prov. The present get, the absent lose. 2. Hāzir men hujjat nahīṅ, gāyab (com. gair ) kī talāsh nahīṅ . Prov. What's present is without dispute, what's absent I'am not going to seek. (You must not look a gift horse in the mouth). 3. Hāzir ho to bhejyo māī! Mendicants. Give what you have, mother! 4. Hathiyā ke devoṅ re mahelwo, ghoṛvā ke harihar dūb, saiyāṅ ke devauṅ bālā jobanvāṅ, kar jorī rahibo hajūr . Bhoj. song. To the elephant I'll give rich cakes, to the horse fresh soft dūb grass, to my spouse my youthful rounded charms, with folded hands 'fore him I'll stand. 2. Ready; prepared; in waiting; coming; at Maiṅ sab tarah hāzir hūṅ . I am at your service. 3. ( Gram .) The second person (S. maddhyam ). hāzir-bāsh , n. m. lit . be ready. A constant or regular attendant; follower; retainer. hāzir-bāshī , n. f. …

(part. act. of حضور) adj. Present, ready, at hand, willing, content; the second person (in grammar). Plur. Arab. حاضرين ḥāẓirīn, Pers. حاضران ḥāẓirān, The people present. حاضر باش ḥāẓir-bāsh (be ready) adj. Content, attendant. حاضر باشي ḥāẓir-bāsh-ī, s. f. Constant attendance. حاضر جواب ḥāẓir-jawāb, adj. Pert, answering wittily, smart. حاضر جوابي ḥāẓir-jawāb-ī, s. f. Repartee. حاضر رهنا ḥāẓir rahnā, To attend, to wait on. حاضر ضامن ḥāẓir-ẓāmin, s. m. Bail security for the appearance of another, a bondsman. حاضر ضامني ḥāẓir-ẓāmin-ī, s. f. 1. The act of giving bail for another. 2. A bail-bond. حاضر و ناظر ḥāẓir-o-nāz̤ir, adj. Present and seeing. حاضر هونا ḥāẓir honā, To be at hand, to be ready, to consent.
subs: s.m. lit. 'Be ready'; a constant or regular attendant, one who is never absent from his post; a follower, retainer
s.f. The act of waiting; constant attendance
adj. Ready with an answer or reply; ready-witted, pert
v.n. To remain or continue present, to be in attendance; to attend, to wait (on)
s.m. Surety for the appearance of another, bail, one bound with the personal surety
s.f. Security for the appearance of another, the act of giving bail for another; bail-bond
(caus. of next), v.t. To cause to be present, cause to appear
v.t. To bring before (one), to bring forward; to deliver up (a person); to introduce, present, to lay before; to make ready, to prepare
adj. Present and seeing (an epithet of the Deity, used in formal oaths, &c.)
v.n. To be present, be in attendance, to attend; to be at hand, be ready, be willing, be at the service of, to consent; to be a witness (of a writing, or event)
adj. Not present, absent, not forthcoming.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian حاضر (hāzir), from Arabic حَاضِر (ḥāḍir).

def: s.m. pl. (of A. ḥāẓir), The people present, those present, those who witnessed (an event, &c.).
Mah. n. f. Invocation of spirit and offerings made to them to beat off an evil spirit to whose shadow one's illness is ascribed. hāz'irāt k. , v. a. To exorcise.
more: s. f. Commanding or imprisoning demons, &c., raising the devil.

r: ḥāẓirāt, ḥāẓirātī, ḥāẓir, ḥāẓirāt karnā, kī
def: = A P حاضراتي ḥāẓirātī, s.f. pl. (of ḥāẓir), Invocation of spirits; exorcising evil spirits; commanding or imprisoning evil spirits; raising the devil
subs: (-kī), To invoke evil spirits, &c.; to exorcise.

def: s.m. An exorciser, a conjurer.

r: ḥāẓirātī, ḥāẓirāt, ḥāẓir, ḥāẓirāt karnā, kī
def: = A حاضرا ḥāẓirāt, s.f. pl. (of ḥāẓir), Invocation of spirits; exorcising evil spirits; commanding or imprisoning evil spirits; raising the devil
more: s. f. Commanding evil spi rits, &c., a conjurer, exorciser.
subs: (-kī), To invoke evil spirits, &c.; to exorcise.

def: s.f. Presence, attendance, appearance (in a court); levee, audience, muster-roll; — an offering of food to a saint or to a departed spirit; a funeral feast (sent to the household of the deceased); — breakfast (of Europeans)
more: n. f. 1. Presence; attendance; appearance (in court). 2. An offering of food to a departed spirit; a funeral feast sent to the household of the deceased (Hin. kaṛvī-khichṛī ). 3. Breakfast. (Europeans). hāzrī bahī, hāzrī kā rajisṭar , A muster-roll; a register of attendance. hāzrī khānā , v. n. To breakfast. hāzrī lenā , v. n. To call over the names; to muster; to take the attendance. hāzrī meṅ khaṛā rahnā , v. n. To be in constant attendance. hāzrīn' , n. m. The persons present; company; assembly; audience. hāzrīn-i-jalsah , The present assembly; this meeting. ba-hāzrī , In the presence of. chhoṭī hāzrī , n. f. The morning tea and toast. (Europeans). naqsha-i-hāzrī o gair hāzrī , A muster-roll; register of attendance.
s. f. 1. Breakfast, desert. 2. Levee, presence, audience, muster-roll. 3. An offering made to a saint. حاضري کهانا ḥāẓirī khānā, To breakfast.
subs: s.f. = ḥāẓrī-kā rajistar, s.m. A register of attendance, a muster-roll
(-kī), To take the attendance, to call out the names (of)
v.n. To be in constant attendance
s.f. 'The little breakfast' (of Europeans), the tea and toast, &c. partaken of (by Europeans) in the early morning (the 'big' breakfast being later on).
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian حاضری (hāzirī), derived from Arabic حَاضِر (ḥāḍir). By surface analysis, حاضر (hāzir) + ی (-ī).

def: s.m. The persons present at the meeting; the present assembly, this meeting.
adj. Low, depressed; — the lower extremity or surface (of anything); a depressed piece of ground, the foot of a mountain; — (in Astron.) the lower apsis in an eccentric orbit (opp. to auj).
more: (from حض) 1. adj. Low. 2. s. f. The lower extremity or surface of any thing. 3. The foot of a mountain. 4. (in astronomy) The lower apsis in an eccentrick orbit (opposed to اوج auj ).

def: n. m. Pl. hazliyāt . 1. Obscene language ( fohsh bāteṅ ). 2. Nonsensical talk.

ety: inf. n. of هزل 'to be jocose; to jest'
def: s.m. Jesting; jest, joke; buffoonery; nonsensical talk; obscene language
more: s. m. A jest, a joke, buffoonery. هزل گو hazl-go, An idle talker.

def: (prop. hazlīyāt, pl. of hazlīyat, abst. s. fr. A. hazlī, rel. n. fr. hazl), s.f. pl. Jokes, jests, pleasantries; nonsensical talk; fiddle-faddle, &c. (see hazl).
more: s. plur. Jokes, jests, nonsensical talk, fiddle faddle.

ety: inf. n. of حزم 'to bind'; 'to make firm'
def: s.m. (?), Strength or firmness of mind or judgment; the being resolved (upon a thing), firm resolution.
more: s. The being resolved (upon a thing) and persevering (in it), firm resolution.

ety: inf. n. of هزم 'to put to flight,' &c.
def: s.m. Putting to flight (an army), rout; flight.
more: s. Putting to flight (an army), flight.

def: n. m. 1. Digestion. 2. Embezzlement. hazm-i-sahīh . A good digestion. hazm k. , v. a. 1. To digest. 2. To embezzle. Sārā māl hazm kar gayā. hazm-i-nāqis . Weak digestion. hazm h. , v. n. 1. To be digested. Sab hazam hai . Street cry. 2. To be embezzled.
s.m. Digesting, concocting; digestion, concoction; — misappropriation, embezzlement
more: s. m. Digestion. هضم کرنا haẓm karnā, 1. To digest. 2. ( met. ) To embezzle.
subs: adj. Causing or aiding digestion, digestive; stomachic
s.m. A sound or good digestion
v.n. To be digested; — to be embezzled.
wety: Borrowed from Arabic هَضْم (haḍm).

ety: for A. هضمية, abst. s. fr. هضمی haẓmī, rel. n. fr. haẓm
def: s.f. The being digestive, digestiveness.
s.m. Forbidding, prohibition; seclusion; — harshness, rudeness.

ety: for A. حضرة, inf. n. of حضر 'to be present'
def: s.f. Presence; — dignity; — a title applied to any great man, the object of resort, your or his Majesty, Highness, Excellency, Eminence, Worship, or Holiness, &c. (when so used respectfully instead of a pronoun or name the gender of the person to whom it has reference is adopted)
more: n. m. 1. Your worship or Highness ( janāb-i-ālī ). 2. Muhammad. hazrat-salāmat , I bow to your Highness.

s. f. 1. Presence. 2. Dignity. 3. Majesty, highness, &c. (a title addressed to the great: and, when used respectfully instead of a pronoun or name, the gender of the person to whom it has reference is adopted). حضرت ظل سبحاني ḥaẓrat z̤ill-i-subḥānī, s. m. His majesty, the shadow of God, the king. حضرت عيسيٰ ḥaẓratīﻌsạ̄, Jesus Christ. (Plur. حضرات).
subs: Greeting to your Highness, &c.; — I bow to your Highness
His Majesty the Shadow of God, the king
s.m. A superior; — a consummate knave or rascal.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian حضرت (hazrat), from Arabic حَضْرَة (ḥaḍra).

def: Jesus Christ. hazrat Maryam , The virgin Mary. baṛe hazrat, baṛe buzurg , 1. A superior. 2. A consummate knave; a perfect scoundrel.
n. m. Raving ( baṛ baṛ , 2).

ety: inf. n. of حظّ 'to be or become fortunate'
def: s.m. Fortune, good fortune, felicity; — share, portion, lot; — pleasure, delight, enjoyment, gratification; flavour, taste
more: n. m. حظ was fortunate. Pleasure; delight; enjoyment; gratification. hazz uṭhā'nā , v. a. To enjoy or delight in. hazz-i-nafsā'nī , Sensual or animal pleasure or gratification. hazz-i-khilāf-i-vaza-i-fitrī , G. G. Unnatural lust. be-haz , adj. Tasteless; insipid; vapid; dry.
s. m. 1. Pleasure, delight. 2. Flavour, taste. 3. Good fortune, felicity. (Plur. حظوظ). حظ نفساني ḥaz̤z̤-i-nafsānī, Sensual pleasures.
subs: (-kā), To partake of the pleasure (of), to enjoy

ety: intens. n. fr. هزل; see hazl
def: adj. & s.m. Very jocose; — a jester, droll, buffoon.
more: adj. (superl. of هزل) Very jocose; s. m. A droll, buffoon.
